dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a diabetic problem at Whitley Gallery.
Hi, I'm Mike. We're here after you were reported to have some altered mental status today.
I don't remember clearly, but my family said I wasn't acting right this afternoon at lunch.
Can you tell us your name and where you are right now, ma'am?
We're measuring your blood sugar now. It reads 56 mg/dL-below normal range for someone who has diabetes.
When did you start feeling unwell, and were you acting normally at lunch before this happened?
I think before I began eating lunch, but I have diabetes and take insulin every day as my medication. I was not myself today at the restaurant.
Do you have any other medical conditions or take other medications aside from insulin? And do you have known drug allergies?
I have hypothyroidism and CHF. I don't have known drug allergies. I take insulin daily with other maintenance medicines. I just can't recall exactly what else I'm on at the moment.
Are you feeling any pain or discomfort, either in your body or mentally right now?
No pain-just feel out of it and sometimes not sure what I'm saying. That seems to be about it.
We'll give you some oral glucose now as part of treatment for hypoglycemia. Please let it stay in your mouth so we can check effects later.
Administered the first dose of 15 grams of oral glucose. She's showing slight improvement in mental clarity now with more response when spoken to.
Her blood sugar is now 65 mg/dL-some improvement but still low for a diabetic episode or crisis, so we'll watch this closely now.
Her blood pressure is 72/41-low, possibly connected to the hypoglycemia and causing dizziness or altered mental status during her meal today.
Her oxygen saturation remains at 100%-no signs of breathing difficulties observed or otherwise reported from her.
Heart rate is 77 beats per minute-steady and not causing any distress or severity at this time.
Based on the hypoglycemia and clinical picture, we're following the hypoglycemia protocol for transport stabilization and emergency care.
Her breathing is 16 per minute and seems regular for her current condition until signs change.
We need to start an IV to give more glucose if needed, but we'll do this with your permission. Can we proceed now?
First IV attempt in an arm vein wasn't successful-very small and difficult visibility, but we'll try the other arm next.
IV starting on the opposite arm now. This attempt also wasn't successful due to vein issues-vascular access is challenging in this condition.
We will give her a second dose of oral glucose now before IV access can stabilize more.
Second dose of 15 grams of oral glucose done now-watching for stabilized alertness and mental return to normal baseline.
Glucose is currently reading 0 on the meter-might be an issue with the equipment, so we will reassess later and conduct validations before transport.
She has a respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute-still within a normal range.
Oxygen saturation still at 99%-still acceptable and likely safe for transport if aware and cooperating during transit.
Blood pressure is 137/77-showing a definite improvement from the initial reading of 72/41 after oral glucose.
Her breathing and heart rhythms still look stable now-no significant breathing problems observed from current condition.
In addition to insulin, have you been receiving oxygen therapy or noted area swelling due to heart condition recently?
Not that I know of-everything has felt fine today before the episode at the restaurant. Weren't feeling any swelling, dizziness or low blood pressure until earlier.
Can you follow this light for us and let us know if your neck hurts or feels stiff at all?
No significant drainage or obstruction in nose, mouth, or throat currently noted.
We'll take you to [COMPANY_NAME] for additional evaluation. That is the appropriate facility for diabetic emergencies.
Ready to carefully place her on the stretcher with proper support-please stand tall ma'am.
Transport position is Fowler's for comfort, viewing, and positioning to support her current condition effectively.
EKG monitoring started for heart observation during transport for early detection of any changes.
Blood pressure remains at 137/77, which is better than the prior low hypotensive readings before glucose.
Heart rate is at 84-stable, trending in the right direction for post-hypoglycemic response.
Her oxygen saturation stays at 99%-no signs of breathing issues at all.
Initial glucose was 56, then 65-reading now 0, possibly due to limitations of meter or error in measurement, but trend shows benefit of provided glucose.
Two unsuccessful IV attempts-oral glucose given in both stages showing acceptable levels after eating but not full normalization yet during transport.
She remains pain-free, although responses are not fully clear due to confusion or mental change from incident.
Patient not reporting any pain or discomfort. Still slightly confused but improving overall in mental clarity and awareness.
She just had lunch at the restaurant-full or minimal prior intake based on her family conversation.
Her GCS remains at 14-consistently oriented although verbal responses are slightly delayed from prior levels. She currently reports no pain.
Protocol clear for hypoglycemia management-next steps begin transfer to receiving facility with oral glucose sufficient to stop further decline.
Patient is now being transported to [COMPANY_NAME]-consistent with protocol for diabetic emergencies.
All monitoring equipment is ready and secured-IV site unchanged, clearing of emergency movement now.
Stretching her into Fowler's with safety locked-ensure comfortable transport while continually monitored.
She was disoriented upon arrival but improved after treatment and now stabilized for transport.
Total of 30 grams of oral glucose given-no IV access obtained successfully in two attempts-no definitive devices or issues seen yet.
Continues to have stable vitals-EKG monitoring now active and continues with clear charting.
Oxygen saturation is at or above 99% continuously, so no emergency treatment required other than transport.
No signs of acute distress observed upon placing in ambulance and prior handover to vehicle.
Documentation and key steps: hypoglycemia, GCS 14, IV attempts, oral glucose, and EKG monitoring noted.
Mental status taken to be slightly less altered-confusion clearing with noticeable alertness.
No IV access yet, vitals responding well to glucose-overall status is stable for transfer to receiving hospital staff.
All relevant notes, medications, and EKG readings shared to receiving nurse for full handover at [COMPANY_NAME].
Stretcher now gripped and locked during travel to hospital-mobility controlled for safety purpose.
Successful handover in progress. Patient is stable and ambulance procedures followed as laid out.
Patient is now safely entering transport to [COMPANY_NAME]-no delays or issues found post-handover.
All steps and documentation completed-final check before leaving the patient with care team at [COMPANY_NAME].
All transport actions followed and procedures applied to relevant standards and practices.
EMS actions and care delivery concluded-patient now under institutional care.
30g oral glucose administered, two IV attempts unsuccessful, and patient status remains improved-disorientation not fully resolved.
Ready for hospital care-awaiting further evaluation by trained medical staff at [COMPANY_NAME].
All documentation curated and procedures completed for pending hospital staff.
30 grams of glucose administered, GCS recorded, and IV siting not successful but patient stable otherwise.
All clinical notes and verbal handover details were completed and provided to the RN at hospital.
All necessary records, actions, and handover steps fully processed and ready.
All clinical moves and notes finalized for transport.
All relevant factual data on file and available for review at hospital.
Logistics of care process were followed stringently and correctly.
All duties completed-no further EMS involvement required here.
